title: State of the Nation Address 2023
sourceLabel: The Presidency of the Republic of South Africa

paragraphs.text: Honourable Speaker, Honourable Chairperson of the National Council of Provinces, Deputy President, Chief Justice, former Presidents, Ministers and Deputy Ministers, Premiers, distinguished guests, fellow South Africans. I address you this evening at a moment of great difficulty for our nation, but also at a moment of great resolve.

The energy crisis is the most pressing constraint on our economy and the daily lives of our people. Load shedding has reached levels never experienced before. It has disrupted businesses, destroyed livelihoods, and tested the patience of every South African. I want to say to the people of South Africa directly: I understand your frustration, and I take full responsibility for leading our country out of this crisis.

Today I am announcing a series of decisive measures to end load shedding and to fundamentally transform our energy landscape. First, I have appointed a Minister of Electricity who will be solely dedicated to overseeing the resolution of the energy crisis. This minister will have the authority and the resources to cut through bureaucratic obstacles and ensure that we bring new generation capacity online as rapidly as possible.

We are accelerating the procurement of renewable energy. Since we lifted the licensing threshold for embedded generation, more than 100 private sector energy projects representing over 9,000 megawatts of capacity are in various stages of development. We are fast-tracking the connections of these projects to the grid. Eskom will be restructured into separate generation, transmission, and distribution entities to improve efficiency, accountability, and to enable fair competition in the energy market.

We are also investing in battery storage, gas-to-power solutions, and the rehabilitation of existing Eskom power stations. The Koeberg nuclear power station’s life extension program is proceeding, which will secure 1,800 megawatts of baseload capacity for another twenty years. These are not long-term aspirations. They are concrete actions with clear timelines, and we will report to the nation regularly on our progress.

The energy crisis has laid bare deeper structural challenges in our economy. South Africa’s GDP growth has been insufficient to reduce unemployment, which remains at unacceptable levels. The expanded unemployment rate stands at over 42 percent. Among young people, it is even higher. This is not merely an economic statistic. It represents millions of lives marked by hopelessness and exclusion. We must confront this with the urgency it demands.

Our Economic Reconstruction and Recovery Plan is being implemented across multiple sectors. We are investing in infrastructure as the primary driver of economic growth and job creation. The Infrastructure Fund has leveraged billions of rand in private sector investment for projects in transport, water, energy, and digital connectivity. We are building roads, bridges, dams, and broadband networks that will underpin economic activity for decades to come.

To the young people of South Africa, I want to say: we hear you, and we are acting. The Presidential Employment Stimulus has created over one million work opportunities since its inception. We are expanding the Youth Employment Service, investing in skills development, and creating pathways from education to employment. The Social Employment Fund is supporting community-based work in areas such as food security, public safety, and environmental restoration.

Crime and violence continue to devastate our communities. The levels of gender-based violence in our country are a national disgrace. We have declared gender-based violence a national crisis, and we are treating it as such. The National Strategic Plan on Gender-Based Violence and Femicide is being implemented with dedicated funding, specialized courts, and a comprehensive victim support system.

We are strengthening the capacity of the South African Police Service. An additional 10,000 police officers will be recruited and deployed to the communities most affected by violent crime. The specialised units focused on extortion, gang violence, and organised crime are being expanded. We are investing in forensic capacity and in the criminal justice system to ensure that those who commit violent acts are brought to justice swiftly and decisively.

Corruption remains a corrosive force that undermines our democracy and steals from the poor. The Zondo Commission laid bare the extent of state capture and its devastating impact on public institutions. We are acting on the Commission’s recommendations. The National Prosecuting Authority has secured convictions and is pursuing complex cases against those who plundered state resources. The Special Investigating Unit has recovered billions of rand in stolen public funds.

Our water infrastructure requires urgent attention. Many municipalities are failing to provide reliable, clean water to their residents. Aging infrastructure, poor maintenance, and inadequate investment have created a looming water crisis that we must address before it becomes as severe as our energy challenges. We are intervening directly in distressed municipalities and investing in water treatment plants, pipeline upgrades, and the development of new water sources.

The state of our railways and ports is a critical bottleneck for economic growth. Transnet’s operational challenges have cost our mining and agricultural sectors billions of rand in lost exports. We are opening the freight rail network to private operators through third-party access agreements. Port operations are being reformed through partnerships with the private sector to improve efficiency and reduce turnaround times.

South Africa’s foreign policy is guided by the principles of solidarity, non-alignment, and the promotion of a just global order. Our decision to bring a case against Israel at the International Court of Justice is a reflection of our deep commitment to international law and human rights. A nation born from the struggle against apartheid will never be silent when we witness the suffering of oppressed peoples anywhere in the world.

As chair of the BRICS grouping, South Africa successfully hosted the BRICS Summit in Johannesburg, which resulted in the historic expansion of the group. This is not about creating blocs or divisions. It is about ensuring that the voices of the Global South are heard in the institutions that shape our collective future. We will continue to advocate for the reform of the United Nations Security Council and the international financial institutions.

Education remains the most powerful instrument for transformation. We are expanding access to early childhood development, improving the quality of basic education, and increasing funding for higher education and technical training. The National Student Financial Aid Scheme is providing fee-free education to students from working-class and poor families, opening doors that were closed for generations.

Our health system is being strengthened in the wake of COVID-19. The pandemic exposed the deep inequalities between our public and private healthcare systems. The National Health Insurance Bill represents our commitment to ensuring that every South African, regardless of their income, has access to quality healthcare. Universal health coverage is not a luxury. It is a right, and we will work to make it a reality.

I want to acknowledge the resilience and determination of the South African people. Despite the load shedding, despite the economic headwinds, despite the crime and the challenges of daily life, South Africans continue to build businesses, educate their children, support their communities, and hold their leaders accountable. This is the spirit that defeated apartheid, and it is the spirit that will carry us through our present difficulties.

Fellow South Africans, the challenges before us are formidable, but they are not insurmountable. We have overcome greater obstacles in our history. What is required now is unity of purpose, decisive action, and accountability at every level of government. I commit to you that this government will work with greater urgency, greater transparency, and greater focus on delivery. Together, we will build the South Africa that our Constitution envisions — a nation of equality, dignity, and opportunity for all. I thank you.
